rationale: The citing paper uses Liu and Min (2020) as a meaningful anchor in its theoretical framing of the joint consumption literature. It is explicitly summarized in Table 1 and cited in the argument that prior joint consumption work typically studies a single shared choice, which the current paper contrasts with its "blended choices" setting. This role goes beyond a passing mention because it helps motivate the citing paper's conceptual gap and contribution, even though the paper does not directly test Liu and Min's specific mechanism.
relation_description: Wight et al. treat Liu and Min (2020) as part of the conceptual foundation for prior joint consumption paradigms and then position their own work as moving beyond that paradigm to sequential parent-child/self choices with possible sharing.
